---
title: "2.7 while Loops"
subtitle: "Part 1: while Loop Intro and Three Loop-Writing Steps"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Loops repeat statements

![Loop overview](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-7-while-loops/loops.png){fig-align="center" width="20%"}

When you play a song, you can set it to loop, which means that when it reaches the end it starts over at the beginning.

A <span class="term">loop</span> in programming, also called <span class="term">iteration</span> or <span class="term">repetition</span>, is a way to repeat one or more statements.

## Control Structures

### Sequence, selection, repetition

If you didn't have loops to allow you to repeat code, your programs would get very long very quickly.

Using a sequence of code, selection (ifs), and repetition (loops), the <span class="term">control structures</span> in programming, you can construct an algorithm to solve almost any programming problem.

## while Loop

### Executes while the condition is true

A `while` loop executes the body of the loop as long as, or while, a Boolean condition is true.

When the condition is false, we exit the loop and continue with the statements that are after the body of the `while` loop.

If the condition is false the first time you check it, the body of the loop will not execute.

## if vs while

### One time versus zero or more times

```java
// The statements in an if run one time if the condition
// is true and zero times if it is false.
if (condition)
{
    statements;
}

// The statements in a while loop run zero or more times,
// determined by how many times the condition is true
while (condition)
{
    statements;
}
```

## while Loop Syntax

### Braces on the AP exam

The `while` statement looks a lot like an `if` statement, but it runs more than once.

The curly braces `{}` are optional when there is just 1 statement following the condition, but required if there are more than 1 statement in the loop.

In the AP exam, they will always use curly braces, which is a good practice to follow.

## App Inventor and Java

### while loop comparison

Almost every programming language has a `while` loop.

![Comparing App Inventor and Java for while loops](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-7-while-loops/whileInAppInventor.png){fig-align="center" width="88%"}

## Repeat Until vs while

### Opposite tests

If you're used to Snap! or Scratch, you may be familiar with the Repeat Until loop.

The `while` test is the opposite of the repeat until test.

If you are repeatedly moving until reaching x position `100`, you must create a Java `while` loop that repeatedly moves while it has <span class="mark">not yet</span> reached x position `100`, or is less than `100`.

## Repeat Until Diagram

### Scratch to Java while

![Comparing Scratch Repeat Until Loop to Java while loop](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-7-while-loops/ScratchRepeatUntilLoop.png){fig-align="center" width="88%"}

## Flow of a while Loop

### Condition before each iteration

Iteration statements change the flow of control by repeating a segment of code zero or more times as long as the Boolean expression controlling the loop evaluates to true.

In while loops, the Boolean expression is evaluated before each iteration of the loop body, including the first.

![Control Flow in a while Loop](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-7-while-loops/WhileLoopFlow.png){fig-align="center" width="30%"}

## Three Steps to Writing a Loop

### Counter-controlled loops

The simplest loops are <span class="term">counter-controlled loops</span>, where the <span class="term">loop control variable</span> is a counter that controls how many times to repeat the loop.

![Three Steps of Writing a Loop](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-7-while-loops/loop3steps.png){fig-align="center" width="44%"}

## Three Steps

### Initialize, test, update

Remember these 3 steps to writing a loop:

::: {.tight-list}
1. Initialize the loop variable before the `while` loop.
2. Test the loop variable in the loop header.
3. Update the loop variable in the while loop body at the end.
:::

## Code Task

### `activecode:: whileloop`

Textbook prompt: Here is a while loop that counts from `1` to `5` that demonstrates the 3 steps of writing a loop. Can you change it to count from `2` to `10`?

## Code Window

### `activecode:: whileloop`

```java
public class LoopTest1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // 1. initialize the loop variable
        int count = 1;

        // 2. test the loop variable
        while (count <= 5)
        {
            System.out.println(count);
            // 3. update the loop variable
            count++;
        }
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: whileloop`

Runestone expects:

```text
2
3
4
5
6
7
8
9
10
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define a loop as iteration or repetition
- explain that a `while` body can execute zero or more times
- compare `if` and `while` control flow
- list initialize, test, and update as the three loop-writing steps
- identify the loop control variable
- change all needed loop parts when changing the count range
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 2

[Next: Tracing and common errors →](2-7-part-2-tracing-and-common-errors.html)
